channel.title: AskYQ - Islamic Rulings from Yasir Qadhi
channel.description: Search fatwas and Islamic rulings derived from Yasir Qadhi's lectures and Q&A sessions.

item.title: Is it permissible for Muslims to celebrate religious holidays of other faiths like Christmas or Diwali?
item.description: Muslims do not celebrate religious festivals of other faiths. However, Muslim converts may attend family gatherings on Christmas for maintaining family ties, as long as they do not participate in religious rituals directed to other than Allah.

item.title: Is it permissible for Muslims to celebrate Halloween?
item.description: Halloween should be avoided because of lingering associations with demons and the supernatural. However, it does not constitute shirk or kufr. Nobody participates as worship. Muslims who participate out of ignorance should be treated with empathy, not harshly judged.

item.title: What constitutes impermissible imitation of non-Muslims (tashabbuh)?
item.description: Prohibited imitation means going out of one's way to adopt something characterizing a civilization of kufr that is not one's own. For Muslims in the West, speaking English, wearing Western clothes, and eating Western food are part of their own culture — not imitation.

item.title: Is the mehndi (henna) ceremony before weddings permissible despite its Hindu origins?
item.description: Mehndi is permissible despite Hindu origins because the religious connotation has been completely lost. It is now purely cultural with no association to Hinduism among Muslims.

item.title: Does a practice having pagan origins make it haram for Muslims?
item.description: Pagan origins alone do not make a practice haram. What matters is the current cultural association. Days of the week named after pagan gods, Olympics, Nike, wedding rings, mehndi — all have pagan origins but are permissible because the association is gone.

item.title: Are personal private celebrations like birthdays, graduations, and promotions permissible in Islam?
item.description: Personal, private, non-religious celebrations are fully permissible. The Sharia is silent about them. As long as activities are halal, the celebration itself is halal. Making something annual (like birthdays) does not make it bid'ah.

item.title: Is it permissible to participate in secular national holidays like the 4th of July?
item.description: Non-religious communal celebrations are cultural matters. At most, it is better for religious institutions not to elevate them, but individual participation (barbecues, fireworks, family gatherings) is permissible.

item.title: Is it permissible for Muslims to celebrate Thanksgiving?
item.description: Thanksgiving is a secular, non-religious holiday. It is more neutral than Halloween with no ritual of worshiping another deity. It is permissible for Muslims to participate.

item.title: Is yoga permissible for Muslims?
item.description: Yoga is permissible in the West where it is purely exercise with no religious connotation, but should be avoided by Muslims in India where it retains Hindu religious associations. This is context-dependent.

item.title: Is abortion permissible for a pregnancy that occurred outside of marriage?
item.description: Abortion before 120 days (4 months) is permissible in cases of pregnancy outside of marriage, as the ruh (soul) has not yet been blown in. Beyond four months, it is generally not allowed as one is dealing with a human life.

item.title: Is taking a conventional mortgage from a bank considered the prohibited riba?
item.description: The riba in mortgages is a secondary type prohibited by hadith, not the primary Quranic riba. Major fatwa councils including the Fiqh Council of North America and European Council permit conventional mortgages under conditions of genuine need. Islamic finance companies are slightly better but essentially similar in substance.

item.title: To what extent can a divorced woman advertise that she is looking for a husband?
item.description: It is permissible for a woman to make it known in a dignified manner. Supported by the hadith of Subay'ah al-Aslamiyyah. Practical methods include word of mouth, Islamic marriage websites, and having a wali or trusted intermediary.

item.title: Is saying "Oh my God" in frustration to parents equivalent to the prohibited "uff"?
item.description: The prohibition is about expressing frustration and contempt toward parents, not the specific word. Whether in English or any language, what matters is the tone and intent. If said with frustration or contempt, it carries the same prohibition.

item.title: Is it permissible to remove a family member from life support or a ventilator?
item.description: Medical treatment including ventilators is mubah (permissible) but not wajib (obligatory). A conscious person has the right to refuse treatment; if unconscious, the family decides collectively. If doctors indicate little to no chance of recovery and the person is in a vegetative state, the majority of scholarly fatawa indicate it is best to let them pass. If the person is young and doctors are optimistic, maintaining support is advisable.

item.title: Does Islamic law dictate specific household chores and roles between husband and wife?
item.description: Islamic law does not dictate specific chore assignments. The Quranic principle is ma'ruf -- treating each other with kindness normal for their time and place. If the couple cannot resolve disagreements, the Quran prescribes that each side appoint an arbiter (hakam). Divorce is a last resort for truly irreconcilable differences.

item.title: How does a married daughter balance obligations between her husband and her parents?
item.description: A married woman's primary obligation shifts to her husband, unlike a son whose priority remains his mother. However, her relationship with parents never ceases. The husband is obligated to be fair and give her reasonable opportunity to care for her parents.

item.title: Is leaving a negative review for a Muslim-owned restaurant considered backbiting?
item.description: A negative factual review is not backbiting. A business review is a public service, not the exposure of a private sin. It is part of accepted norms of commerce.

item.title: Am I obliged to remind a parent suffering from dementia to pray?
item.description: When a person has dementia, the pen is lifted -- they are no longer held accountable. Reminding them occasionally is good but not obligatory. In advanced stages (4th-5th and beyond), they have lost the cognitive capacity required.

item.title: If a husband and wife separate for years without a formal divorce, does the marriage still exist?
item.description: Separation alone does not constitute divorce. If no talaq was uttered or filed, the marriage stands. Supported by the hadith of Zaynab bint Muhammad and Abu al-Aas. If they wish to reconcile, they may resume without a new nikah.

item.title: Can you shorten the prayer (qasr) when traveling for pleasure or vacation?
item.description: By unanimous scholarly consensus, any permissible (mubah) travel qualifies for shortening prayer. No scholar has ever restricted qasr to business travel. Vacation trips qualify.

item.title: Is it permissible for women to hold leadership roles and board positions in the masjid?
item.description: Nothing in the Sharia forbids a woman from being on a masjid board or holding leadership positions. Restricting women from permissible roles risks pushing them away from the community.

item.title: Is it permissible to have a secret marriage in Islam?
item.description: While a secret marriage may be technically valid if the basic conditions are met (wali for a previously unmarried woman, two witnesses, and mahr), it is strongly discouraged and lacks dignity. Not everything that is technically halal is noble or should be encouraged. A marriage conducted in secret is likely to end in pain and disaster, and the bare minimum halal is the lowest level of what is acceptable. Muslims should strive for what is recommended and noble, not merely what is technically permissible.

item.title: How should a Muslim husband balance between his mother and his wife?
item.description: A Muslim husband must simultaneously be a dutiful son and a protector of his wife — he cannot take sides. The Quran commands ihsan (kindness) to parents, not unconditional obedience, which is a critical distinction. Parents do not have the right to interfere in the personal details of their son's marriage. If a mother says something about the wife, the husband absorbs it himself and does not relay it. The husband should minimize direct hostility between the two women, set boundaries, and understand that this tension is a universal, natural part of life across all cultures.

item.title: Does a civil divorce filed by the wife count as an Islamic divorce?
item.description: When a husband files for civil divorce and signs the paperwork, it counts as an Islamic talaq because writing a divorce is the same as verbalizing it. However, when a wife files for civil divorce, the nikah is not automatically dissolved — she must take additional steps. She should either get the husband to agree (making it a khul'), or go to an arbitration committee or knowledgeable imam who can validate her case. If the husband is being unjust, abusive, or has abandoned her, the arbitration committee can issue a faskh (annulment). A wife is not a prisoner in Islam and can ultimately obtain her freedom.

item.title: Is it appropriate for a husband to joke about taking a second wife?
item.description: The Prophet (peace be upon him) never joked about taking a second wife, and doing so is not from the Sunnah. A woman has feelings, and it is not gentlemanly for a husband to provoke those feelings by joking about or dangling the prospect of a second wife. While polygamy itself may be permissible under appropriate circumstances, threatening, teasing, or treating it as humor causes genuine hurt and is unbecoming of a Muslim husband.

item.title: Can a wife leave the house and go to her parents during the iddah period after talaq?
item.description: After a husband gives one talaq, the wife should remain in the same house for the three-month iddah period (three menstrual cycles) as a safeguard Allah has placed to protect the marriage. Leaving the house is strongly discouraged and removes the checks and balances designed to give the couple a chance to reconcile. However, living under the same roof is not a legal condition for the validity of the talaq — the divorce will still take effect after three cycles regardless. The exception is cases of abuse or danger, where leaving is fully permissible.

item.title: How do I make up for years of missed salah after becoming practicing?
item.description: There are two overlapping scholarly opinions on making up years of missed salah. The majority opinion holds that one should make up the missed prayers (qada) by praying extra over the same number of years missed—for example, praying double for 10 years if 10 years were missed, prioritizing qada over sunnah prayers. The minority opinion holds there is no formal qada obligation, but one must still pray extra voluntary prayers as a sign of sincere repentance. Both opinions agree that praying extra is required; the difference is whether the extra prayers are classified as qada or nafl.

item.title: Can you break your salah for a medical emergency in the masjid?
item.description: It is not only permissible but obligatory to break one's salah to attend to a medical emergency. If someone faints or has a medical emergency during congregational prayer, the one or two people nearest should immediately break their salah to help. If a doctor is present in another row, others have every right to walk through the rows and tap that person to break their prayer. Saving a life takes priority over completing salah, which can always be prayed later. Not everyone should break their prayer — only those needed to handle the situation.

item.title: Does saying triple talaq in one sitting count as three divorces or one?
item.description: The correct opinion, which Yasir Qadhi personally holds, is that a triple talaq given in one sitting constitutes only one talaq — not three. This was the position of Ibn Taymiyyah, who was famously jailed for this fatwa. While all scholars agree that giving triple talaq at once is sinful and against the Sunnah, the majority of classical scholars held that it counts as three (making the divorce irrevocable). However, many modern fiqh councils have adopted Ibn Taymiyyah's position because of the practical hardship caused when husbands pronounce triple talaq in anger.

item.title: Can Muslims vote for a candidate who holds some un-Islamic views?
item.description: Muslims should vote for the best available candidate based on key issues — primarily stopping genocide and protecting Muslim civil liberties — rather than expecting any politician to be a perfect Islamic role model. Politicians are not scholars, and voting for a candidate does not constitute endorsement of all their views. The negatives of any Muslim candidate are shared by or exceeded by all other candidates, and penalizing a Muslim for being Muslim when a non-Muslim candidate with the same flaws would be welcomed is counterproductive. Choosing the lesser evil among candidates is the reality of political engagement.

item.title: Is it haram to work for a company that has business ties to Israel?
item.description: Yasir Qadhi differentiates between companies directly aiding and abetting genocide — such as those manufacturing weapons or building factories on occupied land — and generic corporations that merely do some business with Israel. Working for companies directly involved in weapons manufacturing or occupation infrastructure is haram and one should resign. However, the vast majority of corporations (70-80%) have some indirect ties, and declaring all such employment haram is unrealistic and not supported by the Shariah. Boycotting should be done with knowledge, following official BDS lists, not emotional reactions.

item.title: Is it permissible to drink water while standing?
item.description: It is better (mustahabb) to sit down when drinking water if you are able to, but it is not prohibited to drink while standing. Ibn Abbas drank water while standing and said he saw the Prophet do the same. When circumstances make standing natural (water fountain, office water cooler), there is no issue. Sitting is preferred when you are at home and have the luxury to do so.

item.title: What should you do about halal meat standards when visiting someone else's home?
item.description: If you follow a stricter opinion on halal meat, it is permissible to politely and gently ask about your host's meat standards without offending them. This is not questioning their faith, since the disagreement is over scholarly opinions on slaughtering methods, not personal piety. Make light of it, participate in the gathering, and eat vegetarian if needed. Yasir Qadhi personally holds that bismillah is required, stunning is makruh (disliked) but does not render the animal haram, and hand-slaughtered is the safest and best option.

item.title: Can you wipe over regular (non-leather) socks during wudu?
item.description: It is permissible to wipe (masah) over regular cloth socks during wudu; they do not need to be leather. The evidence is that the Prophet once wore socks made of cloth and did masah over them. The only conditions are: (1) the socks must cover the ankles, and (2) you must have had wudu before putting them on. Some other madhhabs are stricter on this point, but Yasir Qadhi follows the position that any sock meeting these conditions suffices.

item.title: Is obedience to parents unconditionally obligatory in Islam?
item.description: The Quran and Sunnah command good conduct (ihsan) toward parents, not unconditional blind obedience. Following Ibn Taymiyyah, obedience becomes obligatory only when three conditions are met: (1) the parents command something that is Islamically good; (2) the request directly benefits them, not matters irrelevant to them like your career or furniture; (3) fulfilling the request does not cause you disproportionate hardship. Even when disagreeing, a child must never be rude and should always use kind words.

item.title: Can you combine the six fasts of Shawwal with making up missed Ramadan fasts?
item.description: You cannot combine the six fasts of Shawwal with making up missed Ramadan fasts under a single dual intention -- they must be done separately. However, you may fast Mondays and Thursdays with the intention of making up Ramadan fasts and still receive the spirit of the sunnah reward for those days. It is better to complete missed Ramadan fasts before starting the six of Shawwal, but it is not strictly required.

item.title: Is vanilla extract permissible in food despite containing alcohol?
item.description: Vanilla extract is permissible in food products. While raw vanilla extract contains about 35% alcohol, no food product flavored with vanilla (ice cream, cake, etc.) will be intoxicating. The prophetic rule is that whatever intoxicates in large but normally consumable quantities is prohibited in small quantities. Since no reasonable amount of vanilla-flavored food can cause intoxication, it is halal. The same ruling applies to nutmeg and similar food extracts.

item.title: Is zakat due on a woman's gold jewelry?
item.description: The safer position is that zakat is due on women's gold jewelry at 2.5%, even if it is worn purely as decoration. While the majority of scholars (three madhhabs) say zakat is not required on gold used for personal adornment, the Hanafi position requires it because gold can function as currency. If following the Hanafi opinion, the woman may sell her smallest piece of jewelry to cover the 2.5% zakat obligation.

item.title: How do you calculate zakat on long-term investments like 401k and mutual funds?
item.description: For long-term investments that you are not actively monitoring or trading (such as mutual funds and 401k accounts), zakat is due on one-third of the total value, not the full amount. So you pay 2.5% of one-third of the portfolio value. However, if you are a day trader actively buying and selling stocks, zakat is due on the full 100% of the portfolio value. This is the position of AMJA and other major Islamic finance councils.

item.title: Are student loans permissible in Islam?
item.description: Student loans involving interest are riba and thus haram by default. However, the European Fiqh Council, AMJA, and the Fiqh Council of North America all permit students to take such loans when no alternative exists — no grants, scholarships, family assistance, or interest-free options — because education constitutes a genuine need (hajah) that is treated like a necessity (darurah). The type of riba in modern bank loans is a lesser, indirect category of riba (forbidden as a means, not in itself), which becomes permissible under genuine need. Subsidized government loans where interest is deferred may not even constitute riba in essence, and should be pursued first before any private bank loan.

item.title: Does haram money become halal when inherited?
item.description: The default is that one does not need to investigate the source of inherited money. However, if one knows for certain that the money was earned through clear-cut haram means (not gray areas where scholars differ), the preferred position is a middle opinion: money that was stolen or unjustly taken from a specific, identifiable person must be returned to them or their heirs. But money that was generically haram (e.g., from riba or selling haram products) where there are no specific claimants — the sin remains on the deceased, and the inheritor is not sinful for taking it, though giving some sadaqah to purify it is recommended.

item.title: Is it allowed for a husband to force his wife into intimacy in Islam?
item.description: A husband does not have the right to force himself upon his wife in Islam. While earlier scholars may have allowed this based on their cultural context and ijtihad, this opinion is not binding because it is not based on explicit Quran and Sunnah. The Quran commands treating spouses with utmost kindness, and the hadith about the wife's refusal indicates the consequence is spiritual (angelic curse) — not a license for the husband to use force. Forcing intimacy causes emotional and physical harm, which violates the Islamic prohibition against causing harm (la darar wa la dirar), and is therefore sinful.

item.title: Should the hijab be enforced by an Islamic government?
item.description: Yasir Qadhi affirms that hijab is a clear religious obligation in Islam according to scholarly consensus, but argues that enforcing it through government coercion is counterproductive and not aligned with the spirit of the Sharia. He emphasizes that Islam's political theory differs fundamentally from Western secularism, that no government is truly morally neutral, and that historically Islamic civilizations did not use morality police to enforce dress codes. He advises focusing on personal piety rather than debating enforcement in distant lands, and advocates for positive encouragement over punitive enforcement.

item.title: What is the history and ruling of the Tarawih prayer?
item.description: Yasir Qadhi explains that 'tarawih' is a term coined by early scholars (not found in the Quran or Sunnah) for qiyam ul-layl performed during Ramadan. He clarifies that qiyam ul-layl is the general night prayer, while tahajjud is the higher category requiring sleeping first and then waking up to pray. Regarding the number of raka'at, the majority of schools recommend 20 (plus witr), the Maliki school recommends 36 (plus witr), and 8 (plus witr) follows the Prophet's personal practice but was never declared mandatory — the controversy over mandating 8 is a modern innovation with no historical basis. Praying at the masjid or at home are both valid, and reading from a mushaf or phone during nafl prayer is permissible according to three of the four schools.

item.title: Is there any leeway in keeping a dog as a pet in Islam?
item.description: Yasir Qadhi explains that the Quran explicitly allows hunting dogs and praises the ability to train them. Regarding the najasah (impurity) of dogs, there are three classical opinions: the Shafi'i/Hanbali view that the entire dog is najis, the Hanafi view that only the saliva is najis, and the Maliki view that nothing about the dog is najis. Regarding ownership, the majority of scholars permit dogs for a legitimate purpose (hajah) such as guarding, hunting, herding, or service/therapy needs. The Maliki school goes further, allowing ownership for any benefit (maslaha). While the majority opinion considers keeping a dog purely as a pet without purpose to be sinful, a strand of the Maliki school considers it merely makruh (disliked, not sinful). Yasir Qadhi personally considers dog saliva to be najis and recommends avoiding keeping dogs without a legitimate need, but acknowledges the broader Maliki position as a valid scholarly opinion.

item.title: Is there one correct 'Sunnah' way to pray salah?
item.description: There is no single 'correct' way to pray that renders all other ways invalid. All four established Sunni schools of law (Hanafi, Shafi'i, Maliki, Hanbali) derive their prayer methods from authentic Sunnah evidence, and the differences between them—such as raising hands during transitions, hand placement, and finger movement in tashahud—are minor and inconsequential. Claiming one particular opinion is the only valid Sunnah while dismissing the other schools as 'rejecting the Sunnah' is incorrect, breeds arrogance, and breaks communal unity.

item.title: Is my job haram if my company has some haram dealings I'm not directly involved in?
item.description: Whether your job becomes haram because your company has some haram dealings is evaluated on five criteria: (1) directness of involvement, (2) your intention, (3) the quantity/proportion of haram, (4) whether this is industry-wide norm, and (5) whether this is your only source of income. Incidental, unintentional, and small-quantity haram that is not your direct responsibility does not make the entire job haram. However, if you are directly employed to facilitate something intrinsically haram, that is clearly impermissible.

item.title: Is wearing the hijab obligatory in Islam and what is the wisdom behind it?
item.description: The hijab (head covering and loose body covering) is obligatory for Muslim women by unanimous consensus of all schools of Islamic law—Sunni and beyond. The Quran explicitly commands this in Surah al-Nur (24:31) and Surah al-Ahzab (33:59), referencing both the khimar (head covering) and jilbab (outer garment). The minimum requirement is that a woman covers her entire body and hair with loose, non-transparent clothing, while the ideal includes an additional outer garment (jilbab). The only area of legitimate scholarly disagreement is on the face covering (niqab). In situations where wearing hijab poses a threat to one's safety, it is permissible to remove it based on the Islamic principle of necessity.

item.title: Why are governments banning the hijab, and what is the Islamic response?
item.description: Hijab bans across Europe, India, and elsewhere are not really about the hijab itself — they are about the hijab as a visible symbol of Islamic civilization in a clash between religious and secular/colonialist worldviews. France's 2010 niqab ban opened a European floodgate. The pattern traces back to French colonization of Algeria (1830s–1960s), where forced unveiling was used as a tool of subjugation. Muslims living in Western democracies must obey the law while not feeling morally obligated to endorse it. The solution is to assert Islamic identity proudly and civically, invoking the very liberal principles these countries claim to uphold.

item.title: Are misyar, secret nikah, and zawaj urfi permissible in Islam?
item.description: Misyar marriage (where the wife waives her rights to financial support and/or regular overnight visits) is technically halal according to the majority of scholars — including Qaradawi and most global fatwa councils — as long as the pillars of nikah are met (wali, mahr, two witnesses, offer and acceptance). However, it is not encouraged and typically ends in divorce, with the woman suffering more. Secret nikah (done with wali and two witnesses but not publicized) is valid but makruh according to three of the four madhabs. Zawaj urfi (unregistered but publicly done) is halal; boyfriend-girlfriend "urfi" with no wali or witnesses is absolutely invalid.
